videos.title: US Stock Market Crashes, April 2025 Easily Understandable
videos.text: In April 2025, the US stock market experienced one of its most dramatic crashes in recent history. Over the course of just a few days, nearly5 trillion dollars in value was wiped out with the Dow plunging more than 4,000 points. So, what exactly happened? What triggered this historic meltdown? And what does it mean for the future of the economy? Let's break it down. It all started with a sweeping set of new tariffs announced by the Trump administration. Branded as Liberation Day, the policy imposed a flat 10% tariff on all imports. Even steeper tariffs were added for goods coming from China, the EU, Japan, and Vietnam. The goal to correct trade imbalances and protect American industries. But the market didn't see it that way. Investors reacted immediately. On April 3rd, the Dow fell nearly 1,700 points, a 4% drop. It was one of the worst single day performances since the COVID pandemic. The S&P 500 and NASDAQ were hit hard, too, especially in the tech sector. Investors began fleeing risky assets and moved to safer investments like bonds and gold. Then came the retaliation. China responded fast, announcing a 34% tariff on all US imports starting April 10th. The message was clear. If the US wanted a trade war, China was ready for it. Other countries followed. The EU planned tariffs up to 50% on US goods. Canada imposed 25% tariffs on 42 billion dollar in imports. Markets panicked. On April 4th, the Dow dropped another 2,200 points. That brought the 2-day loss to over 4,000 points. A staggering collapse, over $4.7 trillion in value, was erased from global markets in just 48 hours. Tech giants, manufacturers, and consumer brands all took massive hits. Fears of a recession started to spread. JP Morgan raised the odds of a global recession to 60%. Citing rising prices, disrupted supply chains, and falling consumer confidence. They warned that the US GDP could shrink if the trade war continues. Despite the turmoil, the government held its ground. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessant called the market drop noise and stood by the tariffs. He claimed the economy would rebound stronger, though few details were offered on how that would happen. Investors didn't wait around. With the US dollar weakening, many began shifting money abroad. Some looked toward European and emerging markets. Others took defensive positions in sectors like energy and consumer staples. So, what comes next? The global economy remains on edge. The risk of a full-blown trade war is still real. Markets are volatile and investor confidence is shaky. In the weeks ahead, economic data will be crucial. Will this be a temporary shock or the start of a deeper downturn? One thing is certain. In a global economy, policy decisions have real consequences. Tariffs aren't just numbers on a spreadsheet. They ripple across borders, markets, and people's lives. Thanks for watching. Be sure to like, subscribe, and stay tuned for more updates on this evolving story. Stay informed and take

videos.title: How Trump's Tariffs are Crashing the Stock Market & Economy - Portfolio Update April 2025 (#40)
videos.text: Donald Trump's latest tariff announcements sparked the worst one-day stock market crash since the pandemic, and it lit the fuse for retaliatory tariffs from other nations, including China, the European Union, and Canada. This sent the global markets into freefall, and the S&P 500 posted back-to-back days of five plus percentage point plunges, erasing trillions of dollars in wealth. But unlike what's been the case in more recent dips, no companies are being spared. The usually unshakable Apple is down over 16% from its close on Wednesday. Meta is down over 14%. Amazon nearly 13%. Netflix 10%. Disney 15%. Starbucks 17%. Texas Instruments 15%. The list goes on and on and on. And we're talking about crucial reliable companies that are the backbone of the economy getting absolutely slaughtered. According to JP Morgan, the likelihood of a global recession in 2025 is now 60%. Up from 40% just a few days ago, which was already up 10% from when Trump took office. And the key word being thrown around here isn't tariffs, it's uncertainty. My name is Paul and I've been investing $100 of real money into Canadian securities each month on this YouTube channel for over 3 years with the goal of highlighting the investment journey of a hypothetical Canadian who will never be able to max out their tax-free savings account. My hypothesis is that people who only have a little bit to set aside at the end of each month can still achieve financial freedom by following a discipline plan of investing what they can on a consistent basis. So, make sure to stick around to see how the journey has been going so far. Now, I'm sure that the vast majority of you are already well aware of these new reciprocal Trump tariffs that have triggered this recent crash. These new tariffs were announced on the 3 almost immediately after the market's close and then on the fourth other countries responded and in many of the important instances these countries responded with their own counter tariffs. Instead of getting into the fairness or sensibility of the specifics regarding these tariffs, I want to instead talk about the impact. To do that, I'm going to be focusing a lot on the word that I previously mentioned, not tariffs, uncertainty. The fact of the matter is that no one knows what Donald Trump or his administration are doing. At this point, I've seen hundreds of different opinions on Trump's plans. From those clutching on to the hope that he's got some secret master plan that he's holding on to that he doesn't want others to know to defectors who are now believing that he tricked the people just to enrich himself and other billionaires like him to longtime critics who are claiming that he's a Russian plant or just a famous narcissist who's gotten in way over his head. Either way, this uncertainty as to what Donald Trump's true intentions and beliefs are leaves a massive void that fills everybody with a feeling of insecurity. See, even if we take the most charitable interpretation of Trump as a master tactician who is using these tariffs as a way to control the world's nations to get them to cave into his terms of building more businesses in the United States and dropping all of their tariffs on US goods, there are still massive strategic oversightes happening. Firstly, it's a huge gamble with not great odds. For instance, if the nations hit with tariffs don't capitulate and instead levy their own counter tariffs, which is what we're seeing from many of the US's major trading partners at the moment, then Americans are going to be stuck with lose-lose trade relationships and worse overall sentiment from other nations that Trump supposedly wants free trade with or business investments from. What's worse is that if the other nations don't back down, it's going to be very difficult for America to go back to the way those relationships were. Unfortunately, Trump has put himself into a position where if he removes the tariffs and asks to go back to the way things were, he'll look weak in front of the whole world. This makes America seem less competent as a nation and will likely embolden US adversaries who will probably conclude that Trump will just back down in the face of adversity. Additionally, there's that uncertainty aspect. Seemingly, no one knows what Donald Trump actually wants or what kind of moves he's willing to pull. And this makes the US as a nation more risky to partner with and to invest in. To give you a quick example, imagine a bunch of your buddies agree to bring a different food over for game night. And the day of, one guy decides he's going to hold the pizza ransom unless everybody else brings extra so that he can take home all the leftovers. And he's not joking. He's serious about it. Even if the guy ends up apologizing and saying that he's sorry and that he doesn't need the leftovers and that he'll share the pizza, the next time around, you're definitely going to be a bit more on edge about trusting him with bringing something instead of being excited about game night and hanging out with friends, eating good food. You're going to be spending time thinking about what scheme this guy's been plotting. And the same goes with the tariff situation. The trust here is gone. Even if Trump removes the tariffs and apologizes, everyone else is still going to be sitting around wondering what he's planning and how he's going to try and screw them over next time. And that lingering negative uncertainty doesn't inspire a desire for investment or partnership. Going back to the game night analogy, most people aren't going to want the guy who tried to pull a fast one on them to be in a position to host game night at his place, especially if that's something that he's been asking for insistently. Similarly, most countries who have been friendly with America in the past aren't now going to want to put more assets into the states after getting slapped with tariffs. Now, one of the popular arguments is that the US is the strongest economy in the world and that it will be able to hold out longer than everybody else and that Trump will force the world to come to the negotiating table. And while that is true, America is the world's largest economy and it would likely be able to throw its weight around to get almost any other country to bend to its whims, Trump hasn't simply pitted himself against a single country. he's facing off against the entire world. For instance, the European Union and China combined make up a larger percentage of the global economy than the United States. Then add on top some of the US's other major trading partners who also are not taking these tariffs lying down and the US is clearly not in a position of power here. Additionally, these tariffs will push nations to build new trade relationships with countries that they might not have even considered partnering with before. For example, Canada and China haven't always seen eye to eye in the past when it comes to the sale of Chinese vehicles in Canada. And that's because of issues regarding the protection of Canadian autoworker jobs, security concerns, and maintaining good auto trade relations with our US partners. Well, if our US partners are harming our relationships by implementing large tariffs that will negatively impact our car manufacturing jobs, maybe Canada will decide to embrace an autotrade relationship with China. The cars would be cheaper and more affordable for Canadians. And China would likely import more Canadian goods as trade relations grow. And as China seeks to replace a lot of what it buys from America, this building of new trade relationships to work around the negative impacts of American tariffs will likely happen with many of the world's major economies over the course of several years. And it's in this way that it is very possible that Trump's attempts to force everyone to come to him will actually end up being what exiles America from the rest of the world. Unfortunately, if this continues to go on long enough, the United States will lose its image as a stable and democratic nation, which in addition to doing less global trade, will mean less of a desire for US currency. And this could spell the end of the US dollar being used as the global reserve currency, which would make the American national deficit a much more serious and risky situation. In reality, things probably won't get that far since polling from before the market crash shows that Donald Trump's approval rating was already dipping well below 50%. And since then, we've even seen US Republican pundits pushing back against the sensibility of this recent round of tariffs. So, we'll probably see the Trump administration change course yet again, if not suffer in the court of public opinion. But uncertainty is the name of the game. So, we'll have to wait and see what is going to happen here. In the meantime, watching the world's largest economy self- emilate is going to be painful for everyone. In the video's intro, you saw some of the major US companies that got fried, but Canadian companies that do business with or source goods from the States are also getting burned pretty badly. Shopify is down 24%. Brookfield is down over 15%, Manu Life down 15%, Canadian National Resources is down almost 13%. And that's just a few of the bigger ones. As for my portfolio's holdings, let's start off with the worst performer. MTYI Food Group is down 8.64% 64% over the course of the month, but the recorded end date for March was as of the market's close on Thursday, and the second leg down of the market crash came on Friday. So, things are actually quite a bit worse than they look here, with shares currently trading below $40. Meanwhile, Goezy was down roughly 4% as of the close on Thursday, but is now trading considerably lower in the $140 range. Lastly, the Morgard North American residential rate appears to have held up pretty well, only dropping by about 1% over the course of the month. But as of recording this, things are again worse than they appear with shares currently trading in the mid $16 range. Yeah, you know it's going to be bad news when the best performer of the month has declined. It was going reasonably well right up until the last day or so. Uh we technically ended the month with $4,053 which is down $75 or 1.83% but that does factor in over $200 in reinvested dividends as part of the book value. But I've only put $3,900 into the account on Wealth Simple. So we're actually technically up by about $153 on my contributed capital at least as the markets end on the 3. But I'm sure that things will look quite a bit worse once we get into the account to invest into this plummeting market. And as you can see here, the benchmarks really didn't fare any better. And again, none of Friday's numbers have been factored in yet. And yeah, it looks really bad. The S&P 500 has already given up huge gains and is right back to within a couple hundred of the competition. Meanwhile, the Canadian equity funds appear to have held up pretty well. They did benefit from a welltime quarterly dividend payout, which helped a bit, but so did the S&P 500, and it's still where it's at. Meanwhile, the Global Value ETF, represented by the pink line, has completely dropped off and won't receive any dividend help until the end of the year. Then we have my portfolio, the green line all the way down at the bottom, and it fared about as well as the two Canadian ETFs. A slight decline, but nothing like the Internationals. Unsurprisingly, this month did not make us any progress towards the red line with the stars, which designates a 12% annual return on investment. But at least we aren't the only ones below the line now. Anyway, the portfolio will need to increase by approximately 15.8% in order to get back in line with our goal. Luckily, drops like we're seeing today that are driven by fear and uncertainty do provide good opportunities to get quality businesses at cheap prices when investing for the long term. And the three companies that I've been buying in the past and will buy again today are all solid profitable securities that are reasonably tariff resistant as I've explained in this video. So with this month's $100 as well as the dividends that I received in March already in the account, I first picked up four shares of the Morgard North American Residential REIT for $16.70 each. Then I purchased.1764 shares of Go Easy at a cost basis of $14168 per whole share. And finally, I picked up an additional 4766 shares of MTI Food Group at a cost basis of $39.40 per whole share. So, as of the markets close on Friday, April the 5th, the portfolio has 175 shares of the Morgard North American Residential REIT worth roughly $2,858, about 18.3 shares of M2I Food Group worth over $715, and 2.76 shares of Goezy, which are worth roughly $392, leaving only 1 cent of cash left in the portfolio after investing. thing. Now, heading over to the dividend tracking chart, March brought in $10.58 coming exclusively from the Morgard North American Residential Reads monthly payout. And that's enough to pay for a bottle of extra strength Tylenol to help ease your pain from looking at the recent losses. Now, thankfully, April is projected to be quite a bit stronger, and we are expecting to receive $1461. And then May, of course, will be the big hitter and should bring in well over $17. But we'll have to wait and see exactly what happens there. As for the portfolio's expected yield, if there are no changes made to the portfolio or its dividend payouts, then we can expect to $173.35 over the next 12 months, which is up 2.8% from last month. And that puts the dividend yield on the money that I've personally contributed to the portfolio at 4.33%. Which is up an additional nine basis points from last month. And that's all for this episode. But before you go, please remember that this video is not financial advice and that I am not a financial adviser. This video is made entirely for the purpose of providing inspiration and insight into my personal investment decisions and it should not be used as a substitute for doing your own research. This video does not have the information required to make good investment decisions on individual securities. So again, I am not a financial adviser. I'm just a Canadian who likes to invest and share my opinions with others. And in doing so, I hope that I was able to provide you with something of value. And if I did, then please consider sharing with anyone else who may also benefit from it. Thanks for watching all the way to the end and enjoy the rest of your day.

videos.title: Stocks hit session lows as President Trump threatens 'massive' tariff hike on China
videos.text: will go to the market and give you the picture here. 12 noon. Good time to take a check on where we are here at the top of the hour. There's the Nasdaq down about 2%. We did reverse lower by now. You know, the headline that the president says the US is calculating a massive increase of tariffs on China over rare earths. China put those new export controls on them. They escalated the trade fight. So that's where we are. It's Eamon Javers who brought us this headline within the last hour. We're going to go back to him now with breaking news for what he has learned here Eamon. >> Well, Scott, it was a fiery social media post just a short time ago from the president. This responding to the Chinese move to put export controls on rare earth minerals yesterday. That's seen as something of an aggressive move by the Chinese against U.S. technology supply chains. Here's what the president posted just a short time ago. He said I was to meet President XI in two weeks at APEC in South Korea. But now there seems to be no reason to do so. He also writes, dependent on what China says about the hostile order that they have just put out. I will be forced, as the president of the United States of America, to financially counter their move for every element that they have been able to monopolize, we have two. He goes on to threaten massive increases in tariffs here, and says that the US administration is considering what its moves might be. So no specific retaliation here yet. Scott. What we've got is a rhetorical firing up of the response from the president, threatening a number of actions, including canceling that summit between Trump and XI Jinping that was expected to happen in South Korea. We don't know if that summit is now canceled or not canceled or what its status might be. We're trying to get that information from the white House right now, but clearly tensions are much higher today than they were yesterday between the United States and China. On the trade front, we'll see what specific moves come from this administration today if they come at all today. >> Scott Eamon, thank you very much. You keep us up to date. Eamon Javers at the Bureau in Washington for us, certainly enough to make the market a little uneasy and Rob, stand up and take notice. It's worth noting as well that prior to today, the S&P had gone 33 sessions without a 1% move. We obviously, for the moment have that down 1.5%. That's largely where you would probably expect to see the weakness within chips. Nvidia, Broadcom, micron, AMD, etcetera and some other names that have what you would consider to be exposure to China. What do you think? >> I think the sharp reaction to this news shows how priced for perfection markets have become, and how, subject to a little bad news they could be. Markets were at 22.9 times as of as of yesterday. You've seen surging optimism looking at the AI, AA surveys. And the markets could potentially move in any risk from tariffs. Let's remember that China tariffs were already at 30%. And you know the the a massive increase would bring rates back up to levels that could be incredibly disruptive. The market is you know, high beta is underperforming today. It's down by 3.7%. Where defensives are outperforming. You're seeing credit underperform. The reality is markets have become solely focused on the good news around AI. And you know, they're ignoring any potential risks from the shutdown incremental weakening in the labor market. And so you get a little piece of news that's unsettling. And you can see a shock like that. >> There's definitely Steph I think to Rob's point been a fair amount of complacency certainly around the issue of trade and tariffs. We haven't mentioned those two t words in a long time because as Rob said, we've been fixated on AI. We've been thinking about earnings and how good they're going to be. And the fact that the fed is cutting interest rates. But we're, you know, certainly reminded right in our faces today that these issues still exist and issue with the most, you know, fierce competitor that we have, I think globally on trade. >> So we're only down 1.3% on this news that I think is encouraging. And I think that is because if you step back. First and foremost, you have to assume that cooler heads will prevail. We have a little bit of time between now and when maybe she and Trump will meet. So we've got probably two weeks of noise. That's okay. We're looking for opportunities to be buying the noise because of all the things you just said better economic growth, decent consumer AI renaissance, good earnings, earnings are going to be very good. And the good news is on Tuesday we start to get earnings. So and which I think the banks are going to be very strong. And you're looking for opportunities though. So to only be down 1.3% in the face of all of this, I think that's actually encouraging. And it speaks to people wanting to buy the dip. And I think that they will and I think they should. >> Well, I mean, it's been a pretty quick reversal as you're looking on on the screen right now. It's it's 1.5%. I mean, there are significant questions, Jay, about kind of where we are not even out, you know, outside of AI and the bubble question which is being asked, you know, every five minutes, right? Obviously. Where's the economy going? Bank of America data today shows what really looks to be two economies. To be quite honest, their data shows lower income consumer spending is growing at 0.6%, higher income growing at four times that. So that's where the the economy is being carried by the high end consumer. And it's being carried by AI, no doubt about it. I mean it's definitely two tapes. It's fully bifurcated in terms of the high end consumer versus the lower end consumer. Top 10% represent 50% of spend, there's no doubt about that. And I think as it relates to what's going on today, this is an absolute buying opportunity from our vantage point. Why. Because GDP is at 3.8%. Yes, we are underway a cutting cycle. We'll see. Kind of we've heard from Hawker today and some other some fed speak this week. I think we will that will be stimulative to the economy. The big beautiful bill will start to run into play over the through the end of this year into next year, which I think will also be stimulative to the economy in in the short run and earnings to Steph's point. We will start in earnest next week on Tuesday with financials which we expect 12% earnings growth. So I like a lot of what's going on right now for the market. This is an opportunity for me. Market was primed anyway for for some level of pullback. Whether this is the beginning of anything who knows. We'll watch. Dow's down almost 600 points. I do think it speaks pretty loudly to the complacency that's been in the marketplace about almost anything outside of the positives to AI, rate cuts, the economy and whatever else you want to throw into that basket. >> During the entire opening of the show, you're the first person to mention rate cuts, and I feel like that's the thing that's in tandem with the AI enthusiasm is when you've got a rate cutting cycle, it's like, what else could the stock market wish for? So to Stephanie and Jason's point, if there's a pullback today, I think there's opportunity there. Kind of pivoting to what you say. Rob. If you look at 22 plus forward earnings, which for old people like us, that's pretty out of control. But it's a handful of names that are buoying the entire markets. So if you lose the AI enthusiasm, Scott, or if the fed backs off of the rate cuts even more so than tariff talk, that could be the reset that I would be a little bit nervous about, where maybe instead of 22 you go down to 20 and now you're six 700 points off the S&P. >> Now you're talking about a game changer scenario for the markets. Those are sort of dramatic risks that would would be out there. And that would have to roll for the market to have a significant problem. I mean, we're you know, even with the pullback we can look at Nvidia. We're still we're not that far away for $5 trillion in market cap. Now this bit of upset is obviously front and center for us as we come on the air for you. So we're at one 189. We need 205. So you know maybe we get that in earnings. Maybe we don't. But that's been one of the stories there. There's you know Morgan Stanley positive on that name AMD's best week since 2016. You've got the Qualcomm news that Mackenzie Sigalos was talking about earlier where China opening this antitrust probe there. So there's just a lot of tension bubbling back over the surface as we see today. Rob, you have Qualcomm Jason does too. I'm just curious as you think about all this playing out, if, you know, the meeting between the president and President XI wasn't supposed to be for a couple of weeks. So if you get, you know, more rhetoric between the two sides, or at least from this end, what that might mean for the market as it looks ahead to a potential meeting between the two. >> You make an important point. What is the breaking of the linkage that we've seen? The linkage between AI, CapEx, equity markets, the US consumer and the US economy. There's a positive and virtuous feedback loop going on. AI capital spend, fuels earnings, earnings, fuel positive stock performance, positive stock performance fuels the large, you know, the wealthiest US consumers that are exposed in the market and that fuels the economy. So any time you're in a see something that challenged the break the break in that link and specifically challenges it in the areas that have done really well, although Qualcomm has lagged quite a bit because of its exposure to China, a year and a half, a situation where you get a dramatic pullback, especially in the names that have participated quite a bit. So I don't think this is broken, but these links will become challenged from time to time. And as long as you think this is still in play and we certainly do through year end, these pullbacks provi

videos.title: I Lost 10k In The April 2025 Stock Market Crash At 19 - Chase Parker On Economy & Trump Tariffs
videos.text: Hello friends. Welcome back to another Parker Speech episode. Um, I'm currently out here at night in the rain. It's very beautiful day. Um, yeah. Into this video, I lost 10 grand in the stock market crash as a 19year-old. Unfortunately, um, due to the Trump tariff announcement. Obviously, this sucks to say because of how proTrump I was. Um, but I asked Chad GBT and Chad GBT said it would make a good episode to record. So, I'm basically going to explain how I lost 10K, what's happening in the current economy. I'm going to explain basic macroeconomics, and I'm basically going to explain what I believe will happen. Right? So, a lot of people that don't know me, my name is Chase Parker. Um, I'm 19. I go to university, also work a full-time job, also have various other hustles. And 2025 has definitely been a very very interesting year so far. Um 2024 a lot of major countries had world elections and we already seeing that play out and we're seeing big differences in the world especially especially with the US economy in general. But yeah basically getting into the stock market crash. Trump announced his tariffs on April what was it? April 2nd 2nd and today's the 6th. So it's been about 4 days. Right now it's about Sunday night. So immediately when he uh announced the tariffs, the S&P 500 literally dropped 10% in a couple days. Like 10% dip in the S&P 500 in a week is crazy. I think it was even more than that. It was like 15%. So currently there is a lot of fear in the markets, a lot of the world markets as well. And typically when the US stock market drops, all markets drop. Everything seems to correlate. There's fear usually in the market. There's uncertainty. So people are selling their shares. are taking out money which causes prices to drop. So, I've I've currently experienced about a 10K loss so far cuz um anyone that's watched watched my hustling course or knows me um I talk about various things that I'm invested into, but basically I had about 15 grand in my Roth IRA, which is pretty much just SPY shares, S&P 500 shares, which is basically just the US stock market in general. And you could just imagine like 15K in an account that experiences a 20% decrease. I had a three grand decrease in there in that account. And obviously um a lot of people that know me know that within the past six months I have been experimenting with um Forex trading algorithms. Can't get the cigarette light. There we go. Can't even get it. Nope. There we go. So, a lot of people that know me know that I've been experimenting with Forex trading algorithms so far this year. So far, I did very, very well. And I've been trading gold and obviously trading other forex pairs. And for January, February, March, my account literally did like 7% per month just trading with a AI robot. So, it did very very well. I think it was up like I was up like four or five grand basically because I put in like 10K in January and I was slowly scaling it up. Anyway, um right when the Trump tariff announcement came, I had like 50 grand in my forex trading account and I had um an open trade on like Australian dollar, Canadian dollar. And as I mentioned, when US markets drop, all markets tend to drop. Forex, usually cryptos followed by that. Even real estate markets are followed by that. So I think when China matched the US US tariffs, that really hit the value of the Australian dollar really hard. So, I think the value of the Australian dollar when I looked at my trade that I got hit with the iPhone storage low notification. So, I had to delete some stuff and then get back to recording. Well, anyway, when the Trump tariff announcement happened and China matched it, that really hit the Australian dollar pretty hard. So, I had a bunch of trades open on Australian dollar. Basically, the Australian dollar went from like 63 to 6. So, from 63 cents to 60 cents in a day, which in a forex market is a very, very big move, especially if you're in long positions. So, basically, I got cooked on some Forex trades. Well, I didn't get cooked. The robot got cooked. The AI algorithm that I was using. Luckily, with everything that I trade, I always use a stop-loss because I've been doing this for years. I'm not stupid and obviously I've had my fair share of losing money. So yeah, I think um that Forex account literally lost like 7K in one day in value just from that announcement. Not necessarily because of the US stock market, but because of it hit other markets as well. So yeah, so I've lost about 10K pretty much due to the markets. And I'm not actually upset. I mean, obviously I'm going to have to make some adjustments in my life right now. Can't get this freaking cigarette. I'm going to have to make some adjustments because obviously I had other ideas for this year like I wanted to buy a rental property. I have other projects that I want to invest to blah blah blah. But now that I'm kind of 10 grand down on equity, my I don't know, my my my spending cash is a bit more limited. So, I'm being very very I guess conservative, having a very conservative approach so far for the rest of this year. So, but a lot of people don't actually understand what is currently happening with this Trump tariff and what it's actually what Trump is trying to do. So basically typically when markets drop and prices go down everything tends to correlate. So say if the US stock market drops house prices practically drop because because people have less money people are investing in less markets people are buying less houses that causes the prices of houses to go down and basically that makes it more affordable for you to buy a home. So let's say with me I want to buy a rental property right say the rental property I want to buy is 250k now but because of this maybe by the end of this year the property I want to buy by the end of this year might be 200 grand. So, yes, I might have lost 10 grand on stocks on paper, but I mean, perhaps if that causes the price of everything else to go down, then perhaps you'll get a 50k discount on something you were already going to buy anyway. So, I mean, it might actually not be the worst thing in the entire world. And obviously, losing money isn't necessarily Trump's fault. Trump can't control people want to withdraw money from their accounts. Obviously, there's a lot of fear and uncertainty in the market. And when there's a lot of fear and uncertainty in the market, people are selling their shares. They're pulling equity out, which causes shares to go down. And that's not necessarily Trump's fault. What Trump is trying to do is he's trying to crash the market essentially on purpose. As I mentioned, lower prices, and that's also going to incentivize the Fed to lower interest rates. So basically, people in the US can borrow money for free to use for businesses, to use for mortgages, to buy property, to buy assets, which is basically going to help the economy grow even better. It's going to help the average US citizen. And on top of um what was it? Lowering the rates.
